title: Oil inventories limit the pain of the Strait of Hormuz closure
gist: Oxford Economics' June World Economic Prospects holds world GDP growth forecasts at 2.4% in 2026 and 3.1% in 2027, little changed month-on-month. It judges that global spillovers from the Strait of Hormuz closure have been moderate because other regions raised oil output and drew down inventories, curbing the supply shock, and expects the global economy to avoid recession despite cuts since the US/Israel war with Iran began.

insights.claim: Oxford Economics forecasts world GDP growth of 2.4% in 2026 and 3.1% in 2027, little changed from a month ago.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: A stable sub-trend global growth baseline anchors expectations and signals no fresh shock-driven downgrade this month.
insights.quote: Our forecasts for world GDP growth of 2.4% in 2026 and 3.1% in 2027 are little changed from a month ago.
insights.locator: Below is just the top-level summary

insights.claim: World growth forecasts were cut significantly since the onset of the US/Israel war with Iran, but Oxford Economics still expects the global economy to avoid recession.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: A no-recession base case despite the war supports staying invested through the geopolitical shock rather than de-risking to cash.
insights.quote: We've cut our world growth forecasts significantly since the onset of the US/Israel war with Iran, but we still think the global economy will avoid a recession.
insights.locator: Our forecasts for world GDP growth

insights.claim: Global spillovers from the Strait of Hormuz closure have been moderate because other regions increased oil output and ran down inventories, curbing the scale of the oil supply shock.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: Inventory drawdowns and spare output are absorbing the shock now, but the buffer is finite, so watch inventory levels for the point pain re-accelerates.
insights.quote: other regions have increased their oil output and run down their inventories, thereby curbing the scale of the oil supply shock.
insights.locator: The economic spillovers to the global economy
